card.indications: Treatment of schizophrenia and bipolar I disorder, including acute manic or mixed episodes and maintenance therapy. It may also be used with fluoxetine for selected depressive episodes associated with bipolar I disorder or treatment-resistant depression.
card.mechanism: Antagonizes dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors, with additional activity at histamine, muscarinic, and alpha1-adrenergic receptors.
card.dosing: Oral dosing is individualized and commonly begins at 5 to 10 mg once daily in adults. IM dosing for acute agitation is formulation specific and requires monitored administration. Use lower starting doses in older, debilitated, or hypotension-prone patients.
card.routeAdministration: Give once daily with or without food. Allow orally disintegrating tablets to dissolve on the tongue. For IM use, follow product-specific reconstitution and monitoring instructions and do not confuse short-acting IM with long-acting injectable products.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to olanzapine or product components. Avoid use in patients with dementia-related psychosis unless no safer alternative exists because mortality and stroke risk are increased.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: increased mortality in older adults with dementia-related psychosis. Risks also include severe metabolic changes, weight gain, hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia, neuroleptic malignant syndrome, tardive dyskinesia, orthostatic hypotension, seizures, anticholinergic effects, and leukopenia or neutropenia.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Somnolence, weight gain, increased appetite, dry mouth, constipation, dizziness, orthostatic hypotension, and restlessness.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome, tardive dyskinesia, severe hyperglycemia or diabetic ketoacidosis, stroke, agranulocytosis, seizures, severe hypotension, myocarditis, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: Alcohol and other CNS depressants increase sedation. Antihypertensives increase hypotension. Anticholinergic drugs increase constipation and urinary retention. Smoking and CYP1A2 inducers may lower concentrations; fluvoxamine can increase exposure.
card.monitoring: Monitor mental status, agitation, suicidality, weight, BMI, waist circumference, fasting glucose, lipids, blood pressure, orthostatic symptoms, abnormal movements, temperature, CBC when indicated, and bowel function.
card.patientTeaching: Report fever with rigidity, severe weakness, uncontrolled movements, fainting, excessive thirst or urination, or severe constipation. Rise slowly and avoid alcohol. Continue diet and exercise measures to limit weight gain.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Monitor calorie intake, weight, glucose, and lipids. Encourage a balanced diet and regular activity as tolerated.

card.indications: Treatment of gastroesophageal reflux disease, erosive esophagitis, peptic ulcer disease, and hypersecretory conditions, and use in selected Helicobacter pylori eradication regimens.
card.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits the gastric parietal-cell hydrogen-potassium ATPase, suppressing basal and stimulated acid secretion.
card.dosing: Common adult regimens range from 20 to 40 mg once daily depending on indication. H. pylori regimens and severe hypersecretory conditions use different schedules. Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest appropriate duration.
card.routeAdministration: Give before meals, usually before breakfast. Swallow delayed-release products whole; some capsules may be opened and sprinkled on approved soft food according to product directions. Do not crush enteric-coated granules.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to substituted benzimidazoles or product components. Concurrent rilpivirine-containing products are contraindicated because reduced gastric acidity can lower rilpivirine absorption.
card.warnings: Long-term or high-dose use may increase risk of C. difficile diarrhea, fractures, hypomagnesemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, acute interstitial nephritis, lupus, gastric polyps, and rebound acid symptoms after discontinuation.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, and gas.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Acute interstitial nephritis, severe hypomagnesemia with arrhythmia or seizures, C. difficile colitis, severe skin reactions, lupus, anaphylaxis, and hepatic injury.
card.interactions: Can reduce absorption of drugs requiring gastric acidity. May reduce clopidogrel activation and increase concentrations of some CYP2C19 substrates. Warfarin, digoxin, methotrexate, tacrolimus, and certain antiretrovirals may require monitoring.
card.monitoring: Monitor symptom response, GI bleeding, magnesium with prolonged therapy or interacting drugs, renal function for unexplained decline, vitamin B12 with long-term use, bone risk, and signs of infection or lupus.
card.patientTeaching: Take before meals and do not crush delayed-release granules. Report persistent diarrhea, black stools, reduced urine, severe rash, muscle cramps, palpitations, or seizures.
card.nutrition: Take before eating. Avoid foods that worsen reflux and use adequate calcium and vitamin D intake if long-term therapy and fracture risk are concerns.

card.indications: Prevention and treatment of nausea and vomiting associated with chemotherapy, radiation, surgery, and selected other causes.
card.mechanism: Blocks serotonin 5-HT3 receptors centrally in the chemoreceptor trigger zone and peripherally on vagal nerve terminals.
card.dosing: Dose depends on indication, route, age, and hepatic function. Severe hepatic impairment limits the total daily dose. Chemotherapy and postoperative regimens have specific timing and dose schedules.
card.routeAdministration: Give oral doses at the prescribed time before emetogenic therapy. Allow orally disintegrating tablets to dissolve on the tongue. Administer IV doses at the recommended rate and monitor high-risk patients for rhythm changes.
card.contraindications: Concomitant apomorphine use because profound hypotension and loss of consciousness may occur, and hypersensitivity to ondansetron or related agents.
card.warnings: Can cause QT prolongation and torsades de pointes, serotonin syndrome, hypersensitivity, myocardial ischemia, and masking of progressive ileus or gastric distention.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, constipation, dizziness, fatigue, and transient liver-enzyme elevation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Torsades de pointes, ventricular arrhythmia, severe bradycardia, serotonin syndrome, anaphylaxis, myocardial ischemia, and severe constipation or ileus.
card.interactions: Other QT-prolonging drugs and electrolyte disturbances increase arrhythmia risk. Serotonergic drugs increase serotonin-syndrome risk. Apomorphine is contraindicated.
card.monitoring: Monitor nausea and vomiting, hydration, bowel function, ECG in high-risk patients, potassium and magnesium when indicated, liver function in severe disease, and symptoms of serotonin syndrome.
card.patientTeaching: Report fainting, palpitations, severe constipation, abdominal swelling, agitation, sweating, tremor, or muscle rigidity. Follow exact timing before chemotherapy or surgery.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Maintain fluids and electrolytes as tolerated and use small meals during nausea.

card.indications: Treatment of overactive bladder with urge urinary incontinence, urgency, and frequency, and selected neurogenic detrusor overactivity.
card.mechanism: Blocks muscarinic receptors in the bladder, reducing involuntary detrusor contractions and increasing bladder capacity.
card.dosing: Immediate-release adult dosing commonly begins at 5 mg two to three times daily. Extended-release products are generally once daily. Transdermal products have formulation-specific schedules. Use lower doses in older adults or patients sensitive to anticholinergic effects.
card.routeAdministration: Swallow extended-release tablets whole. Measure liquid accurately. Apply patches or gel only to clean, dry, intact skin and rotate sites. Avoid exposing gel application sites to others until dry and covered.
card.contraindications: Urinary retention, gastric retention or severe decreased GI motility, uncontrolled narrow-angle glaucoma, and hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Can cause urinary retention, severe constipation, heat prostration from decreased sweating, angioedema, confusion, hallucinations, and worsening glaucoma. Older adults are more susceptible to cognitive and anticholinergic effects.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dry mouth, constipation, dizziness, blurred vision, drowsiness, nausea, and dry eyes.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Urinary retention, intestinal obstruction, severe confusion or hallucinations, angle-closure glaucoma, angioedema, heat stroke, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: Other anticholinergic drugs increase dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, and confusion. CNS depressants increase drowsiness. CYP3A4 inhibitors may increase exposure.
card.monitoring: Monitor urinary frequency and incontinence, bladder emptying, bowel function, mental status, vision, temperature tolerance, dry mouth, and skin reactions with transdermal products.
card.patientTeaching: Use caution driving until effects are known. Prevent overheating, maintain hydration, use oral care for dry mouth, and report inability to urinate, severe constipation, eye pain, facial swelling, or confusion.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Increase fluids and fiber if appropriate to reduce constipation, unless restricted.

card.indications: Management of pain severe enough to require an opioid when alternative treatments are inadequate. Extended-release products are reserved for severe persistent pain requiring continuous around-the-clock therapy.
card.mechanism: Agonizes central mu-opioid receptors, altering pain perception and response while depressing respiratory drive and GI motility.
card.dosing: Dose is individualized by pain severity, prior opioid exposure, formulation, age, and organ function. Opioid-naive patients require conservative starting doses. Extended-release products are not interchangeable with immediate-release products and must be swallowed whole.
card.routeAdministration: Assess pain, sedation, respiratory rate, and oxygenation before and after dosing. Measure liquid with a calibrated device. Do not crush, chew, split, or dissolve extended-release tablets because a potentially fatal dose may be released.
card.contraindications: Significant respiratory depression, acute or severe bronchial asthma in an unmonitored setting, known or suspected GI obstruction including paralytic ileus, and hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include addiction, abuse, misuse, life-threatening respiratory depression, accidental ingestion, neonatal opioid withdrawal, dangerous interactions with benzodiazepines or other CNS depressants, and CYP3A4 interactions.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Constipation, nausea, vomiting, drowsiness, dizziness, itching, sweating, and dry mouth.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Respiratory arrest, profound sedation, severe hypotension, overdose, dependence, withdrawal, adrenal insufficiency, serotonin syndrome, seizures, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: Benzodiazepines, alcohol, gabapentinoids, antihistamines, and other CNS depressants increase fatal respiratory risk. CYP3A4 inhibitors can raise concentrations; inducers can reduce effect or trigger withdrawal. MAO inhibitors and serotonergic drugs require caution.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain relief, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, sedation level, blood pressure, bowel function, misuse risk, urine retention, and signs of overdose or withdrawal.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly as prescribed, store locked, never share, and keep naloxone available when recommended. Avoid alcohol and sedatives. Seek emergency help for slow breathing, extreme sleepiness, blue lips, or inability to awaken.
card.nutrition: Take with food if nausea occurs. Increase fluids and fiber and use a bowel regimen as prescribed. Avoid alcohol and grapefruit products if directed because of interaction risk.
